section.title: Ordinances for Priests, Prince, and Offerings
section.sectionRationale: Now that the glory has returned and the altar is consecrated, the vision turns to who may approach this holy God and how. A shut gate, a purified priesthood, a just prince, and a calendar of atoning feasts all answer one question: how does a defiled people draw near to a holy God without being consumed?
section.thesis: Access to God's restored presence is not casual or automatic; it is carefully ordered around holiness, so that only a consecrated priesthood may serve at His table, only a just prince may lead His people, and only atoning sacrifice may open the way, all of it a shadow pointing to the one High Priest and one sacrifice that actually secures what these ordinances only picture.
section.frameNote: Seven scenes: the shut east gate reserved for the prince; the LORD's rebuke of past defilement and the demotion of the wandering Levites; the consecration and conduct of the priests of Zadok; the LORD Himself as the priests' only inheritance; the holy portion of land set aside for sanctuary, priests, Levites, and city; just weights and a just prince; and the appointed feasts of atonement, Passover and Booths.

units.label: The shut east gate
units.note: only the prince may sit there

verses.text: The man then brought me back to the outer gate of the sanctuary that faced east, but it was shut.

And the LORD said to me, “This gate is to remain shut. It shall not be opened, and no man shall enter through it, because the LORD, the God of Israel, has entered through it. Therefore it will remain shut.
Only the {{nasi|prince}} himself may sit inside the gateway to eat in the presence of the LORD. He must enter by way of the portico of the gateway and go out the same way.”

units.label: Enough of your abominations
units.note: past defilement rebuked, the Levites who strayed

verses.text: Then the man brought me to the front of the temple by way of the north gate. I looked and saw the glory of the LORD filling His temple, and I fell facedown.

The LORD said to me: “Son of man, pay attention; look carefully with your eyes and listen closely with your ears to everything I tell you concerning all the statutes and laws of the house of the LORD. Take careful note of the entrance to the temple, along with all the exits of the sanctuary.
Tell the rebellious house of Israel that this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘I have had enough of all your abominations, O house of Israel.

In addition to all your other abominations, you brought in foreigners uncircumcised in both heart and flesh to occupy My sanctuary; you defiled My temple when you offered My food, the fat and the blood; you broke My covenant.
And you have not kept charge of My holy things, but have appointed others to keep charge of My sanctuary for you.’
This is what the Lord GOD says: No foreigner uncircumcised in heart and flesh may enter My sanctuary, not even a foreigner who lives among the Israelites.
Surely the Levites who wandered away from Me when Israel went astray, and who wandered away from Me after their idols, will bear the consequences of their iniquity.

Yet they shall be ministers in My sanctuary, having charge of the gates of the temple and ministering there. They shall slaughter the burnt offerings and other sacrifices for the people and stand before them to minister to them.

Because they ministered before their idols and became a stumbling block of iniquity to the house of Israel, therefore I swore with an uplifted hand concerning them that they would bear the consequences of their iniquity, declares the Lord GOD.
They must not approach Me to serve Me as priests or come near any of My holy things or the most holy things. They will bear the shame of the abominations they have committed.
Yet I will appoint them to keep charge of all the work for the temple and everything to be done in it.

units.label: The priests of Zadok, consecrated to serve
units.note: vestments, marriage, teaching, judging, holiness of life

verses.text: But the Levitical priests, who are descended from Zadok and who kept charge of My sanctuary when the Israelites went astray from Me, are to approach Me to minister before Me. They will stand before Me to offer Me fat and blood, declares the Lord GOD.
They alone shall enter My sanctuary and draw near to My table to minister before Me. They will keep My charge.
When they enter the gates of the inner court, they are to wear linen garments; they must not wear anything made of wool when they minister at the gates of the inner court or inside the temple.
They are to wear linen turbans on their heads and linen undergarments around their waists. They must not wear anything that makes them perspire.

When they go out to the outer court, to the people, they are to take off the garments in which they have ministered, leave them in the holy chambers, and dress in other clothes so that they do not transmit {{qodesh|holiness}} to the people with their garments.
They must not shave their heads or let their hair grow long, but must carefully trim their hair.
No priest may drink wine before he enters the inner court.
And they shall not marry a widow or a divorced woman, but must marry a virgin of the descendants of the house of Israel, or a widow of a priest.
They are to teach My people the difference between the holy and the {{chol|common}}, and show them how to discern between the clean and the unclean.
In any dispute, they shall officiate as judges and judge according to My ordinances. They must keep My laws and statutes regarding all My appointed feasts, and they must keep My Sabbaths holy.
A priest must not defile himself by going near a dead person. However, for a father, a mother, a son, a daughter, a brother, or an unmarried sister, he may do so,
and after he is cleansed, he must count off seven days for himself.
And on the day he goes into the sanctuary, into the inner court, to minister in the sanctuary, he must present his {{chatat|sin offering}}, declares the Lord GOD.

units.label: The LORD Himself is their inheritance

verses.text: In regard to their inheritance, I am their inheritance. You are to give them no possession in Israel, for I am their possession.
They shall eat the grain offerings, the sin offerings, and the guilt offerings. Everything in Israel devoted to the LORD will belong to them.

The best of all the firstfruits and of every contribution from all your offerings will belong to the priests. You are to give your first batch of dough to the priest, so that a blessing may rest upon your homes.
The priests may not eat any bird or animal found dead or torn by wild beasts.

units.label: The holy portion of the land

verses.text: “When you divide the land by lot as an inheritance, you are to set aside a {{terumah|portion}} for the LORD, a holy portion of the land 25,000 cubits long and 20,000 cubits wide. This entire tract of land will be holy.
Within this area there is to be a section for the sanctuary 500 cubits square, with 50 cubits around it for open land.
From this holy portion, you are to measure off a length of 25,000 cubits and a width of 10,000 cubits, and in it will be the sanctuary, the Most Holy Place.

It will be a holy portion of the land to be used by the priests who minister in the sanctuary, who draw near to minister before the LORD. It will be a place for their houses, as well as a holy area for the sanctuary.
An adjacent area 25,000 cubits long and 10,000 cubits wide shall belong to the Levites who minister in the temple; it will be their possession for towns in which to live.
As the property of the city, you are to set aside an area 5,000 cubits wide and 25,000 cubits long, adjacent to the holy district. It will belong to the whole house of Israel.

Now the prince will have the area bordering each side of the area formed by the holy district and the property of the city, extending westward from the western side and eastward from the eastern side, running lengthwise from the western boundary to the eastern boundary and parallel to one of the tribal portions.
This land will be his possession in Israel. And My princes will no longer oppress My people, but will give the rest of the land to the house of Israel according to their tribes.

units.label: Just weights, a just prince
units.note: honest measures and the prince's provision for worship

verses.text: For this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘Enough, O princes of Israel! Cease your violence and oppression, and do what is just and right. Stop dispossessing My people, declares the Lord GOD.’
You must use honest scales, a just ephah, and a just bath.
The ephah and the bath shall be the same quantity so that the bath will contain a tenth of a homer, and the ephah a tenth of a homer; the homer will be the standard measure for both.
The shekel will consist of twenty gerahs. Twenty shekels plus twenty-five shekels plus fifteen shekels will equal one mina.
This is the contribution you are to offer: a sixth of an ephah from each homer of wheat, and a sixth of an ephah from each homer of barley.
The prescribed portion of oil, measured by the bath, is a tenth of a bath from each cor (a cor consists of ten baths or one homer, since ten baths are equivalent to a homer).

And one sheep shall be given from each flock of two hundred from the well-watered pastures of Israel. These are for the grain offerings, burnt offerings, and peace offerings, to make atonement for the people, declares the Lord GOD.
All the people of the land must participate in this contribution for the prince in Israel.

And it shall be the prince's part to provide the burnt offerings, grain offerings, and drink offerings for the feasts, New Moons, and Sabbaths, for all the appointed feasts of the house of Israel. He will provide the sin offerings, grain offerings, burnt offerings, and peace offerings to make atonement for the house of Israel.

units.label: The appointed feasts: Passover and Booths
units.note: atonement for the sanctuary and the people

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD says: ‘On the first day of the first month you are to take a young bull without blemish and purify the sanctuary.
And the priest is to take some of the blood from the sin offering and put it on the doorposts of the temple, on the four corners of the ledge of the altar, and on the gateposts of the inner court.
You must do the same thing on the seventh day of the month for anyone who strays unintentionally or in ignorance. In this way you will make atonement for the temple.
On the fourteenth day of the first month you are to observe the Passover, a feast of seven days, during which unleavened bread shall be eaten.
On that day the prince shall provide a bull as a sin offering for himself and for all the people of the land.
Each day during the seven days of the feast, he shall provide seven bulls and seven rams without blemish as a burnt offering to the LORD, along with a male goat for a sin offering.
He shall also provide as a grain offering an ephah for each bull and an ephah for each ram, along with a hin of olive oil for each ephah of grain.
During the seven days of the feast that begins on the fifteenth day of the seventh month, he is to make the same provision for sin offerings, burnt offerings, grain offerings, and oil.’

nasi.gloss: Prince, chief, one lifted up or exalted. Deliberately not melek, king; this coming ruler serves under the LORD's own kingship rather than replacing it, a picture completed in the true Son of David who reigns only as the Father's appointed King.

qodesh.gloss: Holy, set apart, consecrated for God's use alone. The priests' vestments carry this holiness so carefully that it must not even brush against ordinary people outside the sanctuary.

chol.gloss: Common, ordinary, profane in the neutral sense of not set apart. Teaching Israel to tell qodesh from chol, the holy from the common, was central to the priest's calling, Leviticus 10:10.

chatat.gloss: Sin offering, purification offering. Even a consecrated priest entering to minister after any defilement must bring this offering, a constant reminder that no one, however set apart, approaches God on the ground of his own cleanness.

terumah.gloss: A contribution, an elevated or set-apart portion lifted out from the whole and given to the LORD. The holy district of land is a terumah, marked off from the ordinary inheritance of the tribes as belonging to God alone.

basic.context: The glory has returned and the altar has been consecrated in chapter 43. Now the vision answers the question that return provokes: who may draw near, and how? A gate is permanently shut because the LORD Himself passed through it. Israel's past sins, foreign hands defiling the sanctuary and priests who wandered after idols, are rebuked and answered with careful new arrangements: only the line of Zadok, who stayed faithful, may serve at the altar and the table, wearing garments that keep holiness from leaking out to ordinary life. The chapter also gives the priests no land, because the LORD Himself is their portion, marks out holy ground for sanctuary, priests, Levites, and city, corrects the abuses of former princes with a call to just weights and just rule, and lays out feasts of atonement, Passover and Booths, that keep the whole community right with God across the year.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prophetic vision, not architectural blueprint or a legislative code meant for slavish, literal rebuilding. Interpreters differ on how far to press the details of priestly succession, land measurements, and especially the sacrifices, but the underlying message is plain and applies to every reader: coming near to a holy God cannot be casual, it requires atonement, consecration, and a mediator who has not defiled himself, all of which Israel had failed and all of which is fulfilled in Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: The coming ruler is called nasi, prince, never melek, king, a deliberate demotion under the LORD's own kingship. The priests must teach the difference between qodesh, holy, and chol, common, and even their consecrated service still requires a chatat, sin offering, on the day they minister. The holy ground itself is a terumah, a portion lifted out and set apart for the LORD alone.
basic.typology: The priesthood of Zadok, faithful when others strayed, anticipates the one true High Priest who never wandered and needs no sin offering for Himself. The shut east gate, reserved for the prince alone, anticipates Christ's unique access to the Father. The prince's duty to provide atoning sacrifices for the whole house of Israel anticipates the greater Prince who offers not bulls and goats but Himself.
basic.christ: Hebrews 7 says our High Priest is holy, innocent, undefiled, separated from sinners, needing no daily sacrifice first for His own sins as these priests did, then for the people's, since He did this once for all when He offered up Himself. Where the sons of Zadok needed a sin offering merely to approach on the day of their own ministry, Christ never required one. And where these ordinances image right access to God through careful mediation, Hebrews 10:19-22 says believers now have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus, through a new and living way that He opened for us.
basic.god: God is a God of order, not casualness, He fixes exactly who may serve, what they may wear, whom they may marry, and how atonement must be made, because His holiness cannot be approached carelessly. Yet He is also a God who provides generously for those He calls near, giving the priests not a strip of land but Himself as their inheritance, and giving Israel, through the prince's provision, a whole calendar of ways to be reconciled to Him.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 10:10 sets the priests' charge to distinguish holy from common, clean from unclean, echoed at Ezekiel 44:23. Numbers 18:20 already declares that the LORD is the priests' portion and inheritance, echoed at 44:28. Hebrews 7:26-27 contrasts a priesthood that must offer sacrifices for its own sins with Christ, who has none. Hebrews 10:19-22 grounds believers' bold access to God in Christ's blood rather than in priestly qualification.
basic.oneStory: From Eden onward the Bible traces one long question: who may approach the holy God and live? Here that question receives an elaborate, careful answer in shadow form, a screened priesthood, special garments, repeated atonement, and a prince who provides sacrifices for the people. Every part of that careful order finds its answer in Jesus Christ, our great High Priest, who is Himself the perfect priest, the true Prince, and the final sacrifice, so that all who trust Him may now draw near with a clean heart and full assurance of faith.

context.literary: Follows directly from the glory's return and the altar's consecration in chapter 43, turning from the presence of God to the ordered access of His people, priests, and prince; this long block of instruction fills out the remainder of the temple vision before it climaxes in the river and the land in chapters 47-48.
context.historical: Addressed to exiles whose own recent history included foreign hands defiling the sanctuary, and the ordinary Levitical priesthood mixing worship of the LORD with idols; the rebuke of 44:6-14 answers those specific, remembered failures with a fresh, careful arrangement meant to prevent their repetition.
context.canonical: Draws directly on Leviticus and Numbers, Leviticus 10:10's charge to distinguish holy from common, Numbers 18:20's declaration that the LORD is the priests' inheritance, while anticipating the book of Hebrews, which reads this whole system of careful, repeated mediation as a shadow of the single, sufficient priesthood of Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show exiles who had watched God's presence depart in judgment exactly how a holy God may now be safely and rightly approached, through a purified priesthood, a just prince, ordered land, and atoning feasts, so that access to His restored presence would never again be treated carelessly.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific prophetic vision of priestly and civic order for God's dwelling with His people; it is not a legislative code meant to be mechanically reinstituted today. Its abiding truth is that a holy God must be approached through atonement and consecrated mediation, truths now fulfilled rather than repeated in Christ.

debates.question: Do the sin offerings and burnt offerings of 45:18-25 describe a future, literal reinstitution of animal sacrifice?

views.label: A future literal altar and offerings
views.case: Some read this as picturing sacrifices literally reinstituted at a future temple, understood as memorial or covenantal acts honoring what Christ has done rather than offerings that themselves atone, since the passage's language and detail read most naturally as concrete instructions.

views.label: A vision of ordered holiness fulfilled in Christ
views.case: Others read these details as a symbol-rich picture of atonement and access, whose entire substance is already fulfilled in Christ's once-for-all sacrifice, so that no future offering of blood is ever again needed or intended, Hebrews 10:1-14.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that Christ's finished work is the reality these offerings only pictured; interpretive humility is appropriate, but neither view treats this passage as diminishing the sufficiency of the cross.

debates.question: Why are foreigners uncircumcised in heart and flesh barred, while the passage still allows for their inclusion elsewhere in the vision, 47:22-23?

views.label: A strict, ethnic-and-ritual boundary
views.case: The plain sense bars anyone uncircumcised, whether by birth or by heart, protecting the sanctuary from the very mixing of pagan practice that defiled it before, 44:7.

views.label: A picture of true, not merely formal, belonging
views.case: The pairing of heart and flesh suggests the real barrier is unbelief and impurity of heart, not ethnicity as such, which is why chapter 47 can already picture believing foreigners receiving a full inheritance among the tribes.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage insists that only those truly consecrated, not merely present, may approach, a principle the New Testament applies to circumcision of the heart in Christ, Romans 2:28-29.

study.originalLanguages: The coming ruler is nasi, prince, never melek, king, always subordinate to the LORD's own reign. The priests are charged to teach the difference between qodesh, holy, and chol, common, yet even their own consecrated service on the day they minister requires a chatat, sin offering, showing that consecration does not remove the need for atonement. The land itself is measured out as a terumah, a portion lifted out and set apart entirely for the LORD.

typology.type: The priesthood of Zadok, faithful when the Levites strayed
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the true and greater High Priest, holy, undefiled, separated from sinners, who never wandered and therefore needs no sin offering of His own, Hebrews 7:26-27.

typology.type: The east gate shut because the LORD passed through it, opened only to the prince
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the unique access of Christ, the true Prince, who alone opens for His people the new and living way into God's presence, Hebrews 10:19-20.

typology.type: The prince's provision of atoning sacrifices for the whole house of Israel
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the greater Prince who provides not bulls and goats offered again and again but His own single, sufficient offering, Hebrews 10:11-14.

study.christ: Hebrews 7:26-27 says our High Priest is holy, innocent, undefiled, separated from sinners, and unlike these priests, who needed a sin offering merely to enter and minister, He had no need to offer sacrifices first for His own sins and then for the people's, since He did this once for all when He offered up Himself. Where these ordinances pictured careful, repeated mediation as the only way to approach a holy God, Hebrews 10:19-22 declares that believers now have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus, through a new and living way that He opened for us through the curtain, that is, through His flesh.
study.god: God is a God of exacting order: only a specific priestly line may serve, wearing specific garments, keeping specific rules of marriage and mourning, because His holiness cannot be approached on human terms. Yet within that order He is remarkably generous, giving the priests not land but Himself as their inheritance, and giving the whole house of Israel, through the prince's provision, a repeated, gracious means of atonement rather than leaving them to bear their guilt alone.

crossReferences.note: Sets the priests' foundational charge to distinguish the holy from the common, echoed directly in Ezekiel 44:23.
Already declares that the LORD Himself, not land, is the priests' portion and inheritance, echoed in Ezekiel 44:28.
Contrasts a priesthood that must repeatedly offer sacrifice, first for itself, with Christ, who has no such need and offered Himself once for all.
Grounds believers' confident access to God in the blood of Jesus and the new, living way He opened, not in priestly lineage or ritual qualification.

study.oneStory: Since Eden the Bible has traced one question: who may approach the holy God and live? Here that question receives an elaborate answer in shadow, a screened priesthood, holy garments, repeated atonement, a prince who provides sacrifice for his people. Every strand of that careful order points forward to Jesus Christ, the perfect and undefiled High Priest, the true Prince who alone passes through the shut gate, and the final sacrifice who needs no repetition, so that all who trust Him now draw near with a clean heart and full assurance of faith.

quiz.q: Why must the east outer gate of the sanctuary remain permanently shut?

options.text: Because the LORD, the God of Israel, entered through it, so it must remain shut
options.note: 44:2 gives this exact reason; the gate is set apart because of who passed through it.

options.text: Because it had fallen into disrepair and was never rebuilt
options.note: No disrepair is mentioned; the reason given is theological, not structural.

options.text: Because foreigners had broken it down in the earlier siege
options.note: The text gives no such account; the gate's closure is a fresh, deliberate provision.

options.text: Because only Levites, never priests, may use that gate
options.note: The gate's use is restricted to the prince alone, not simply reserved from Levites.

quiz.q: What happened to the Levites who wandered after idols when Israel went astray?

options.text: They were expelled from the sanctuary entirely and given no role
options.note: They are not expelled outright; they retain a demoted but real role in the sanctuary.

options.text: They kept full priestly access exactly as before, since all was forgiven
options.note: The text is explicit that they bear consequences and lose access to the altar and holy things.

options.text: They bear the consequences of their iniquity by serving in a demoted role, without priestly access to the altar or the most holy things
options.note: 44:10-14 describes exactly this demotion: real service, but not priestly access.

options.text: They became the new ruling priestly family in place of Zadok's line
options.note: The opposite occurs; Zadok's line is elevated precisely because the Levites strayed.

quiz.q: According to 44:28, what is the priests' inheritance in the land?

options.text: A double portion of land compared to the other tribes
options.note: The text explicitly denies them a possession in Israel at all.

options.text: The LORD Himself is their inheritance and possession; they receive no land
options.note: 44:28 states this directly, echoing Numbers 18:20.

options.text: Only the tithes, with a normal land allotment besides
options.note: They do receive offerings and tithes, but the text stresses they get no land inheritance at all.

options.text: Whatever land the prince chooses to grant them each year
options.note: Their inheritance is fixed as the LORD Himself, not a variable grant from the prince.

quiz.q: What does the Lord GOD demand of the princes of Israel in 45:9?

options.text: That they increase the size of their own royal landholdings
options.note: The demand is the opposite, an end to seizing land and oppressing the people.

options.text: That they cease violence and oppression and do what is just and right, ending their evictions of the people
options.note: 45:9 records this rebuke almost word for word.

options.text: That they appoint new priests of their own choosing
options.note: Priestly appointment is not the subject of this rebuke; just rule and honest dealing is.

options.text: That they abolish the office of prince altogether
options.note: The office continues with real duties; the rebuke reforms rather than removes it.

quiz.q: How does Hebrews 7 contrast Christ with the priesthood pictured in this passage?

options.text: Christ is holy, innocent, undefiled, and unlike these priests has no need to offer sacrifice first for His own sins before offering for the people
options.note: Hebrews 7:26-27 makes exactly this contrast, since Ezekiel's priests need a sin offering merely to minister, 44:27.

options.text: Christ needed the same seven-day consecration process before He could serve
options.note: Hebrews stresses the opposite, that Christ's priesthood required no such repeated consecration or sin offering.

options.text: Christ serves only as a Levite, in the line demoted in this chapter
options.note: Hebrews 7 explicitly notes Christ descends from Judah, not Levi, and holds a different, greater priesthood.

options.text: Christ's priesthood adds a further sacrifice to complete what Zadok's line began
options.note: Hebrews teaches Christ's single sacrifice completes and ends the need for further offerings, rather than adding to them.

questions: The shut gate reminds Israel that not every way of approaching God is open to just anyone. Where might you be tempted to treat access to God as casual, rather than remembering what it cost to open the way through Christ?

The priests are given no land, because the LORD Himself is their inheritance and possession. What would change in your life if you actually believed God Himself, not what He gives you, is your greatest reward?

God rebukes the princes for oppression and evictions, and calls for honest scales and just measures. Where in your own dealings, work, money, or leadership, might God be calling you to greater justice and honesty this week?

Even the consecrated priests needed a sin offering before they could minister on the day of their service. How does remembering that no one, however set apart, approaches God on the ground of their own cleanness deepen your gratitude for Christ's finished sacrifice?

The Levites who strayed after idols lost priestly access but were not cast out entirely; they kept a real, if diminished, place of service. Where do you need to trust God's discipline as serious but not final, if you have wandered and are returning to Him?

translationNotes.note: 'You offered My food, the fat and the blood' pictures the sacrifices as food set on the LORD's table, language carried through into 44:16, 'draw near to My table'; the flattened em dash in the source text has been rendered here as a comma without changing the sense.

'Uncircumcised in heart and flesh' pairs an outward, bodily mark with an inward, spiritual condition; interpreters differ on whether the ban targets uncircumcised foreigners specifically or, more broadly, anyone lacking true devotion to the LORD regardless of birth.
An embedded line break in the source text between 'his possession in Israel' and 'And My princes' has been flattened into continuous prose here; no words were added or removed.
The parenthetical note on the cor and homer measurements is original to the verse's explanatory style, clarifying that a cor and a homer are equivalent units, each containing ten baths.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 44:1-45:25. Em dashes at 44:7, 44:9, 45:17 replaced with commas; an embedded line break within 45:8 flattened into continuous prose. No em or en dashes used elsewhere. Web search this session (Truth For Life, Desiring God, Gospel Coalition, Gospel in Life) found no sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) that specifically expounds Ezekiel 44-45; only broad, multi-book overview messages surfaced, so sermon status is none.
